nativeRange.habitat: Shallow wave-exposed reef flats, reef crests, and intertidal zones; occasionally found in marginal habitats

decorPreferences: open placement with spacing from neighbors

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae. Supplemental feeding with zooplankton, phytoplankton, and small meaty particulates has been reported to benefit growth and polyp extension. Supplementation with calcium, strontium, iodine, and trace elements is generally advised; notably, iron supplementation should be avoided as the skeleton naturally sequesters iron salts for its characteristic blue coloration.

avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals with sweeper tentacles placed directly adjacent

summary: Heliopora coerulea is the sole living fossil octocoral with a calcified aragonite skeleton — uniquely blue from iron salts it sequesters. CITES Appendix II. Advanced reef coral; needs high flow, high light, and stable water chemistry.
careNotes:
Heliopora coerulea is a remarkable biological outlier: it is the only surviving member of order Helioporacea and the only octocoral (eight-tentacled coral class also containing soft corals like Xenia and gorgonians) that builds a true massive calcified skeleton. Despite having a rigid skeleton that resembles SPS (stony) coral externally, it is classified as a soft coral based on its octocoral anatomy — eight pinnate tentacles per polyp, characteristic of the subclass Octocorallia.

The iconic sky-blue skeleton color results from the coral sequestering iron salts from surrounding seawater into its fibrocrystalline aragonite skeleton. This pigmentation has allowed fossil identification across the Cretaceous period, making it a true living fossil. Note: do NOT add supplemental iron to the aquarium — the coral naturally manages iron uptake, and excess iron can be harmful to the overall reef ecosystem.

Taxonomic note: Wikipedia (2026) lists the order as Scleralcyonacea; traditional taxonomy uses Helioporacea. The family Helioporidae is monotypic — Heliopora coerulea is its only species.

Key care considerations:
• Lighting: Generally advised to provide 150–300 PAR. Reeflings reports the species can adapt to 50–150 PAR but higher-light placement supports stronger growth. Start frags at lower PAR and acclimate upward.
• Flow: High, chaotic water movement is generally advised. Direct laminar flow to polyps should be avoided. The species is found naturally on wave-exposed reef crests.
• Water chemistry: Requires stable calcium (380–430 ppm), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), and magnesium (1250–1400 ppm). Swings should be avoided. Unlike SPS corals, this species is somewhat more forgiving of minor parameter fluctuations due to its soft-coral physiology.
• Growth forms: This species exhibits variable morphology — branching, encrusting, plating, and tubular forms are all known. In the aquarium it tends to encrust over rocks and glass. Wild colonies can reach 70–100 cm in height/diameter but aquarium frags typically grow slowly to 20–30 cm over several years.
• CITES Appendix II: International trade is regulated. Aquacultured frags are the recommended and most commonly available source for the hobby. Wild-collected specimens are subject to CITES export documentation.
• Placement: Middle to upper rockwork is generally advised to maximize light exposure.
• The species has been reported to lack sweeper tentacles, making it less aggressive toward neighboring corals than most LPS species. However, standard spacing from neighbors is still advised to allow natural encrusting growth.
• Conservation: Listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List; threatened by bleaching, ocean acidification, habitat destruction, and collection pressure.
breedingNotes: Fragging is possible by cutting or breaking branches; the exposed skeleton tends to heal and encrust over time. Sexual reproduction in the wild occurs via broadcast spawning. Deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby. Aquacultured frags are commercially available and preferred over wild-collected specimens due to CITES Appendix II status.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – CCGC Aquacultured Blue Ridge Coral
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provides temperature 72–78°F, salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, lighting moderate to high, flow medium to strong, care level moderate.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Heliopora coerulea
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Helioporidae, class Octocorallia), biology (blue skeleton from iron salts, only octocoral with massive skeleton, living fossil since Cretaceous), native range (Indian and Pacific Oceans), CITES Appendix II, IUCN Vulnerable. Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: AquariumDomain – Blue Ridge Coral
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 72–79°F (22–26°C), salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, flow moderate to strong, placement middle to high, care level easy. Noted lack of sweeper tentacles.

additional.name: Reeflings – Blue Coral (Heliopora coerulea) Complete Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked lighting (50–150 PAR reported, though higher recommended), temperature 24–28°C, flow moderate, care level moderate to easy. PAR range lower bound informed decision to note acclimation range in careNotes.

additional.name: FreshMarine – Blue Ridge Coral
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 72–83°F upper bound, salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.10–8.40, high intensity lighting, strong water current, peaceful temperament. Iron supplementation warning sourced here.
